---
title: "Statistical plots"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 8
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch7 — Plotting with Matplotlib"
  - "M2 Ch11 — Mean, variance & standard deviation"
learning_objectives:
  - "Read a histogram as the shape of one variable's distribution"
  - "Read a boxplot for the median, the spread, and any outliers"
  - "Read a scatter plot for the relationship between two variables"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 18
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
  - ../../lib/data.py
  - data/penguins.csv
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: histogram-shows
    q: "What does a histogram show?"
    a: "The distribution of one variable — its values grouped into bins, with bar heights showing how many records fall in each bin, so you can see the centre, the spread, and the shape (symmetric or skewed)."
  - id: boxplot-shows
    q: "What does a boxplot show?"
    a: "A five-number summary of one variable: the box spans the first to the third quartile (the middle 50%) with a line at the median; the whiskers reach the rest of the typical range; and points beyond the whiskers are flagged as outliers."
  - id: scatter-shows
    q: "What does a scatter plot reveal?"
    a: "The relationship between two variables — one per axis, one dot per record. An upward band means they rise together (positive), a downward band means one falls as the other rises (negative), and a shapeless cloud means little relationship."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

A column of 342 numbers tells you almost nothing when you stare at it. `.describe()`
helps — a mean here, a standard deviation there — but a table of summary statistics
still asks you to imagine the data. A *picture* hands it to you directly. One glance
at the right chart and you know whether your penguins cluster around a typical weight,
how spread out they are, and whether two measurements move together.

This chapter is about three workhorse charts that answer three different questions:

- A **histogram** answers *"what does one column look like?"* — its distribution.
- A **boxplot** answers *"where is the middle, how wide is the spread, anything weird?"*
- A **scatter plot** answers *"do these two columns move together?"* — a relationship.

We'll read all three from the **Palmer Penguins** dataset you already met: 344 birds
measured at Palmer Station, Antarctica. Each plot is a few lines of Matplotlib on top
of a pandas column, and the skill we're after is less about the code and more about
*reading the result*.

> You'll need from before: drawing axes with Matplotlib (M3 Ch7), and the idea of a
> mean, variance, and standard deviation as the centre and spread of data (M2 Ch11).
> Matplotlib loads itself the moment you `import matplotlib.pyplot`, so there is
> nothing to install.

---

## A histogram: the shape of one distribution

A column of body masses ranges from light birds to heavy ones, but how are the values
*spread out* in between? A **histogram** answers that. It chops the range into equal
slices called **bins**, counts how many values fall in each bin, and draws a bar of
that height. The result is the **distribution** — the silhouette of the data.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A histogram of penguin body mass — its distribution in 12 bins"
#| fig-alt: "Histogram with body mass in grams on the x-axis from about 2700 to 6300 and the number of penguins on the y-axis. Bars rise to a peak in the 3500 to 4000 gram range, then tail off gradually toward the heavier masses on the right."
from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
mass = df["body_mass_g"].dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(mass, bins=12, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("body mass (g)")
ax.set_ylabel("number of penguins")
ax.set_title("Distribution of penguin body mass")
plt.show()
```

Read the shape from left to right. The bars are **tallest around 3500–4000 g**, so
that is where most penguins sit — the *centre* of the distribution, near the mean of
about **4202 g**. The bars then **trail off to the right**, toward the 6000 g birds,
making a longer tail on the heavy side than the light side. That mild lean is called a
**right skew**, and it is exactly why the mean (4202 g) sits a little *above* the
median (4050 g): a few heavy penguins pull the average up. The takeaway: this column
has a single hump centred near 4200 g, a typical range of roughly 3000–5500 g, and a
gentle tail toward heavier birds.

The **bin count matters**. Too few bins and you blur real detail into one fat bar; too
many and random wobble looks like structure. Twelve bins is a reasonable starting
point here; nudging it up or down a little is a normal part of looking at data.

## A boxplot: median, spread, and outliers

A histogram shows the whole shape. A **boxplot** is more compact: it draws a
*five-number summary* so you can compare the centre and spread of a column at a glance.

The pieces are worth naming. The **box** spans the **first quartile (Q1)** to the
**third quartile (Q3)** — the middle 50% of the data, a range called the
**interquartile range (IQR)**. The **line inside the box** is the **median** (the
middle value). The **whiskers** stretch out to the rest of the typical data, reaching
at most 1.5 × IQR past each quartile. Any point beyond a whisker is drawn on its own
and flagged as a possible **outlier**.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A boxplot of body mass — the box is the middle 50%, the line is the median"
#| fig-alt: "Vertical boxplot of body mass in grams. The box spans roughly 3550 to 4750 grams with a median line near 4050 grams. Whiskers extend down to about 2700 and up to about 6300 grams, with no individual points drawn beyond the whiskers."
from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
mass = df["body_mass_g"].dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.boxplot(mass)
ax.set_ylabel("body mass (g)")
ax.set_title("Body mass: median, spread, and outliers")
plt.show()
```

Read it from the middle outward. The median line sits near **4050 g** — half the
penguins are lighter, half heavier. The box runs from about **3550 g (Q1)** to
**4750 g (Q3)**, so the middle 50% of birds span an IQR of roughly **1200 g**. The
whiskers reach down to the lightest bird (≈ 2700 g) and up to the heaviest (≈ 6300 g),
and crucially **no points are drawn past the whiskers** — by the 1.5 × IQR rule, this
column has *no flagged outliers*. The takeaway: a typical penguin weighs about 4050 g,
the central half of the flock falls within a 1200 g band, and nothing is extreme
enough to stand out as anomalous.

That last point is what makes boxplots a first stop in data cleaning: a stray dot far
above the top whisker is your cue to check for a typo or a genuinely unusual record
before it skews everything downstream.

## A scatter plot: a relationship between two variables

The histogram and boxplot each describe **one** column. A **scatter plot** describes
**two** at once: put one variable on the x-axis, the other on the y-axis, and draw one
dot per record. The *pattern* of the cloud tells you whether the two move together.

Penguins offer a natural pair: do birds with longer flippers also weigh more?

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Flipper length vs body mass — each dot is one penguin"
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot with flipper length in millimetres on the x-axis from about 170 to 230 and body mass in grams on the y-axis from about 2700 to 6300. The dots form an upward-sloping band from lower-left to upper-right, so longer flippers go with heavier birds."
from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
sub = df[["flipper_length_mm", "body_mass_g"]].dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.scatter(sub["flipper_length_mm"], sub["body_mass_g"], s=10, alpha=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel("flipper length (mm)")
ax.set_ylabel("body mass (g)")
ax.set_title("Flipper length vs body mass")
plt.show()
```

The dots form a clear **upward-sloping band** from lower-left to upper-right: penguins
with longer flippers tend to be heavier. That is a **positive relationship**, and a
strong one — the correlation here is about **r ≈ 0.87**, close to the maximum of 1.0,
which is why the cloud looks almost like a fuzzy line rather than a shapeless blob. The
takeaway: flipper length and body mass rise together tightly enough that knowing one
gives you a good guess at the other.

Read scatter shapes like a vocabulary:

- **Upward band** → positive relationship (both rise together).
- **Downward band** → negative relationship (one falls as the other rises).
- **Shapeless cloud** → little or no relationship.

One caution worth carrying forward: a relationship is **not** proof that one variable
*causes* the other. Longer flippers do not *make* a penguin heavy; both grow together
because bigger birds are bigger all over. "Correlation is not causation" is a phrase
you'll meet again — a scatter shows the *what*, never the *why*.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **histogram** shows the **distribution** of one column — bin the values, draw
   bar heights, and read the centre, the spread, and the shape (symmetric or skewed).
2. A **boxplot** shows a five-number summary of one column: the **box** is the middle
   50% (Q1–Q3), the **line** is the **median**, the whiskers reach the typical range,
   and points beyond them are **outliers**.
3. A **scatter plot** shows the **relationship** between two columns: an upward band is
   positive, a downward band is negative, a shapeless cloud is none.
4. Penguin body mass centres near **4050–4200 g** with a mild right skew, and flipper
   length vs body mass is a strong positive relationship (**r ≈ 0.87**).
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`. The
first two use tiny hand-made lists so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

A histogram and a boxplot both lean on the centre and spread of a column. Write
`mean_and_std(values)` to return a tuple `(mean, std)`, each rounded to 2 decimals,
using NumPy's **population** standard deviation. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_mean_std
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_mean_std
import numpy as np

def mean_and_std(values):
    """Return (mean, population std) of values, each rounded to 2 decimals."""
    a = np.array(values)
    return ____  # TODO: (round(float(a.mean()), 2), round(float(a.std()), 2))

run_tests([
    ("simple", mean_and_std([2, 4, 6, 8]), (5.0, 2.24)),
    ("flat", mean_and_std([5, 5, 5]), (5.0, 0.0)),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`a.mean()` is the average and `a.std()` is the population standard deviation. Wrap
each in `float(...)`, then `round(..., 2)`, and return them as a tuple
`(rounded_mean, rounded_std)`. The std of `[2, 4, 6, 8]` is `sqrt(5) ≈ 2.236`, which
rounds to `2.24`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def mean_and_std(values):
    a = np.array(values)
    return (round(float(a.mean()), 2), round(float(a.std()), 2))
```

`np.array(values)` turns the list into a NumPy array; `.mean()` averages it and
`.std()` gives the population standard deviation (dividing by `n`, not `n − 1`).
`float(...)` makes the types predictable and `round(..., 2)` trims each to two
decimals, so `[2, 4, 6, 8]` returns `(5.0, 2.24)`.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

A histogram is, at heart, *counting how many values land in each bin*. Write
`count_in_range(values, lo, hi)` that returns how many values fall in one bin —
between `lo` and `hi` **inclusive** of both ends. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_count_range
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_count_range
def count_in_range(values, lo, hi):
    """Count how many values satisfy lo <= v <= hi (one histogram bin)."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(sum(1 for v in values if lo <= v <= hi))

run_tests([
    ("middle bin", count_in_range([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 2, 4), 3),
    ("none", count_in_range([1, 2, 3], 10, 20), 0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Loop over the values and keep the ones where `lo <= v <= hi` is true. A generator
expression `sum(1 for v in values if lo <= v <= hi)` counts them; wrap it in
`int(...)` so the return type is a plain integer.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def count_in_range(values, lo, hi):
    return int(sum(1 for v in values if lo <= v <= hi))
```

The chained comparison `lo <= v <= hi` is `True` only when `v` is inside the bin
(both ends included). Summing `1` for each match counts them, and `int(...)` returns
a plain integer — so `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` has 3 values in `[2, 4]`.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and draw
**either** a histogram **or** a boxplot of `body_mass_g`, then describe the
distribution in a `print` statement: where is it centred, how spread out is it, and is
there any skew?

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Loads penguins with `load_csv` and plots `body_mass_g` as a histogram or a boxplot.
- Describes the centre (median ≈ 4050 g, mean ≈ 4202 g) and the spread (most birds
  roughly 3000–5500 g).
- Comments on the shape — a mild right skew, since the mean sits above the median.

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Select the column with `df["body_mass_g"].dropna()`, then call `ax.hist(mass, bins=12)`
for a histogram or `ax.boxplot(mass)` for a boxplot. For the description, read the
centre off `mass.mean()` and `mass.median()` and compare them: a mean above the median
signals a right skew.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
mass = df["body_mass_g"].dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(mass, bins=12, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("body mass (g)")
ax.set_ylabel("number of penguins")
ax.set_title("Distribution of penguin body mass")
plt.show()

mean, median = round(mass.mean(), 1), mass.median()
print("centre: mean", mean, "g, median", median, "g")
print("spread: most birds roughly 3000-5500 g")
print("shape: mild right skew, because the mean sits above the median")
```

The histogram has a single hump near 3500–4000 g and a longer tail toward heavier
birds. Because the mean (≈ 4202 g) sits above the median (4050 g), a few heavy
penguins pull the average up — the signature of a mild right skew.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch08.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now turn a column into a picture and *read* it: a histogram for the shape of a
distribution, a boxplot for the median and spread and any outliers, and a scatter for
the relationship between two variables. That trio is the visual core of exploratory
data analysis — the looking-before-modelling step every project starts with. Next we
close the module by pulling these habits together into a small end-to-end analysis.
